---
title: "Strategy & practice"
---

# Strategic considerations

## Choosing where to submit

A rough decision procedure, adapted to our subfield:

1. **Is the primary contribution a theorem?** Aim AoS / JRSS-B / Biometrika / COLT / JMLR (theory track).
2. **Is the primary contribution a methodology with theoretical grounding?** Biometrika / JRSS-B / JASA T&M / Biometrics / Biostatistics / IJB / J. Econometrics / JMLR / AISTATS.
3. **Is the primary contribution a substantive applied result with methodological development?** JASA A&CS / AoAS / Epidemiology / Biometrics.
4. **Is the primary contribution a conceptual or identification argument?** Epidemiology / J. Causal Inference / Observational Studies.
5. **Does the paper speak to clinical trials / regulatory / pharma?** Statistics in Medicine / Pharmaceutical Statistics / Biometrics.
6. **Is the paper about algorithms for causal estimation in complex data?** NeurIPS / ICML / AISTATS / CLeaR, then JMLR expansion.

## R&R strategy

Most papers at good venues go through at least one R&R. Some notes:

- **Take the R&R seriously.** A genuinely responsive revision with a well-written response letter often turns a borderline paper into an acceptance.
- **Don't underestimate the response letter.** It is sometimes read more carefully than the revised paper.
- **Rejections are not terminal.** A paper rejected from JRSS-B often lands at Biometrika or JASA T&M with the reviewers' comments addressed. Many canonical papers in our field were rejected multiple times before finding a home.

## The preprint norm

In our subfield, **arXiv (stat.ME, stat.ML, stat.TH) is the default** — posting a preprint at submission is standard. This applies to journal submissions and is required for most ML conferences. Your advisor's preference rules here, but expect to post.

## Special issues

Special issues are often an underappreciated opportunity, especially for early-career researchers:

- **Editorial board oversight is similar to regular issues** for the top journals
- **Acceptance rates are sometimes higher** because the guest editors actively solicit
- **Turnaround can be faster** due to hard deadlines
- **Visibility can be higher** because special issues are promoted and read as a set

Worth checking the calls-for-papers pages of *Biometrics*, *Biostatistics*, *JRSS-B*, *Stats in Medicine*, and the ML journals annually.

## Reading vs. submitting

Some venues are primarily "reading venues" — places where you keep up with the field even if you rarely submit there:

- **Statistical Science** — read the review articles carefully; they often define how the field understands its own subfields
- **Annals of Statistics** — read the theoretical foundations of whatever you're working on, even if your own contributions target more methodological venues
- **Epidemiology** — read the conceptual papers to understand how methodology translates to substantive practice
- **NeurIPS/ICML causal sessions** — keep up with ongoing developments; even if you don't submit, the field is moving fast

---

# Further practical guidance

## Timing and the tenure clock

A six-year tenure case at an R1 biostatistics department typically requires **10–20 published papers** with the candidate as first or senior author, including **several in A-tier or better venues**, plus **external funding** and **citation evidence**. Concretely, placements at IJB, Biometrics, Biostatistics, JASA, and JRSS-B count, with the exact expectations varying by institution. Ask your chair and senior colleagues.

**Practical implication:** Plan for paper pipelines that can tolerate 1–2 R&R cycles per paper. If you have one paper and it is stuck in R&R limbo, you are exposed. If you have five papers at various stages, you are safe.

## Co-authorship norms

In biostatistics methodology, first-author papers dominate for junior researchers. Advisor typically appears as last author. For collaborative applied work, author order and position become more complicated and should be discussed explicitly with collaborators *before* submission — ideally before the work starts.

## Reviewing as a learning practice

**Volunteering to review (or co-reviewing with your advisor) is one of the most valuable ways to understand what makes a paper succeed or fail.** Ask your advisor to forward you a review they've agreed to take on. Compare your draft review with theirs afterwards.

Many journals in our field are actively trying to broaden their reviewer pool; once you have a publication or two, you can be suggested as a reviewer via ScholarOne profiles.

## Mentorship

No tier list substitutes for the judgment of a good advisor or senior mentor. **Before submitting any paper, have at least one senior person in the field who is not a co-author read a draft.** They will often see gaps and framing issues that you and your co-authors have become blind to.

---

# Acknowledgments and caveats

This document reflects the conventional wisdom of biostatistics and causal inference researchers as of 2026. Tier lists are opinions, not facts. Journal landscapes evolve — *Journal of Causal Inference* barely existed a decade ago; IJB was viewed differently before its open-access period stabilized; CLeaR and TMLR (Transactions on Machine Learning Research, not covered here but worth tracking) are new enough that their standing is still being established.

Treat this as a starting point. Disagree with it. Talk to your advisor. Update it as the field changes.

---

*For corrections, additions, or disagreements, contact the author or open an issue on the shared repository.*
